ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Rasam. Nearest parent is a thin spiced broth or soup. A new concept covering South Indian rasam is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_description: Spiced thin broth, South Indian (closest parent)

main_ingredients: toor dal water (thin strained water from cooked toor dal)
rasam powder (coriander, cumin, black pepper, red chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of rasam is 1 bowl (approximately 200g) — a thin, watery, intensely sour and peppery broth served alongside idli, dosa, or poured over rice.

nutrient_source.title: Rasam — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, derived from component ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN does not have a standalone rasam entry. Values are derived from ICMR-NIN data for tamarind, tomato, toor dal (thin water), and standard rasam spice blend. Rasam is primarily water (~90%) with minimal calorie density.

gi_source: GI testing not applicable — rasam is a very low-carbohydrate broth (approximately 9g carbohydrate per 200g serving) and does not raise blood glucose meaningfully.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 570mg per bowl (200g) — elevated; rasam is traditionally quite salty and sour. Home preparation allows sodium reduction.
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should request reduced-salt rasam or limit to 100ml. The high potassium from tamarind and tomato provides some offsetting benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely low calorie and carbohydrate content — approximately 56 kcal and 9g carbohydrate per full bowl; GI testing not applicable.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rasam is ideal for diabetic patients — very low in calories and carbohydrates. Black pepper in rasam may improve insulin sensitivity. Patients can consume freely.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Black pepper (piperine), turmeric, and tamarind have potent anti-inflammatory properties; rasam is one of the most medicinal foods in South Indian cuisine.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Highly recommended for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Traditionally consumed for joint pain and respiratory conditions. Black pepper enhances turmeric bioavailability. Contains ghee which provides fat-soluble vitamin benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rasam is a thin, intensely sour and peppery South Indian broth — often described as South India's answer to soup — made from tamarind extract, tomato, thin toor dal water, and a rasam masala powder heavy in black pepper, coriander, and cumin. A ghee tadka of mustard seeds, cumin, curry leaves, and garlic is added at the end. Rasam is much thinner and more acidic than sambar. It is consumed as a soup, poured over rice as part of a South Indian meal, or drunk as a digestive remedy for cold and congestion. Extremely low in calories, it is one of the most beneficial drinks in traditional South Indian food culture.

confidence_reason: No standalone rasam entry in ICMR-NIN or HPB. Values estimated from standard rasam recipe ingredient proportions using ICMR-NIN component data. High water content (~90%) makes rasam caloric estimates relatively straightforward.
notes: Contains ghee (small amount) — not vegan; dairy allergen present at trace levels. Cholesterol approximately 3mg per 200g bowl from ghee. GI and GL are listed as null as rasam is a non-meaningful GI test subject — too low in carbohydrates. Sodium values may vary significantly between restaurants and home cooking (250–500mg per 100ml is typical restaurant range).
